summary: O'Neil reflects on her disappointment with political polling after the 2020 election, admitting she fell for polls that told her what she wanted to hear. She argues that consuming polling data is more of an emotional addiction than genuine scientific inquiry. The post links to her Bloomberg Opinion column calling for Americans to break their dependence on political polls, suggesting that doubling down on polling methodology won't help the country understand itself better.
key_points: Political polling functions as an emotional addiction rather than a source of genuine understanding
Polls tend to tell people what they want to hear, creating a feedback loop of confirmation bias
The solution isn't better polling methodology but rather breaking the addiction entirely
money_quotes: I cannot believe I fell, once again, for the polling that gave me the information I wanted to hear.
It is indeed an emotional addiction, rather than a scientific curiosity.
I think we'd all be better off shedding our addiction to political polling.
key_insight: Political polling has become an emotional crutch that confirms our biases rather than a tool for genuine understanding, and we should treat it like an addiction to be broken.
